title: Five Numbers Every Small-Business Owner Should Check on Their P&L Each Month
excerpt: Learn the five P&L numbers every small-business owner should review monthly, how to calculate them and which operating decisions each one should trigger.
coverAlt: Small-business profit and loss report with five key financial signals highlighted

structuredData.headline: Five Numbers Every Small-Business Owner Should Check on Their P&L Each Month
structuredData.description: Learn the five P&L numbers every small-business owner should review monthly, how to calculate them and which operating decisions each one should trigger.
keywords: how to read a profit and loss statement

content:
Each month, check five signals: revenue trend, gross margin, operating expense ratio, operating profit margin and cash coverage. Together they show whether demand is changing, each sale is becoming more valuable, overhead is controlled, operations are producing profit and the business has enough liquidity to keep executing its plan.

What should a small-business owner look for on a P&L?

A profit and loss statement, or P&L, summarizes revenue earned and expenses incurred during a period. It answers a vital question: did normal operations create a profit or a loss? A useful monthly review goes further. It compares the current month with prior periods and the budget, converts major lines into percentages of revenue and ends with a specific operating decision.

Begin only after the books are reasonably complete. Confirm that sales, payroll, vendor bills, inventory adjustments and accruals belong to the right period. Also confirm whether the report uses cash or accrual accounting. Under accrual accounting, income is generally reported when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of when cash is received or paid, as IRS Publication 538 explains. The right basis depends on how the business keeps its books. Consistency is essential: a comparison is misleading if the accounting method or account classifications changed halfway through it.

Then read the statement from top to bottom. Start with demand, test the value of each sale through gross margin, inspect overhead, calculate operating profit margin and finally connect profit to available cash. Compare against last month, the same month last year and the operating plan. A budget-versus-actual review gives each variance context: it distinguishes an intentional investment from an unexpected drift.

A worked monthly example

Consider this simplified P&L. It uses the same four lines throughout the guide so that every ratio reconciles to the statement.

LineThis monthLast month
Revenue$100,000$92,000
Cost of sales$40,000$34,000
Operating expenses$48,000$44,000
Operating profit$12,000$14,000

This month, revenue is $100,000 and total operating costs are $88,000: $40,000 of cost of sales plus $48,000 of operating expenses. The difference is $12,000 of operating profit. Last month, revenue was lower at $92,000, yet operating profit was higher at $14,000. That tension is the point of a ratio-based review. Growth alone did not translate into more operating profit.

The example is deliberately simple. A real statement may show returns, discounts, several cost-of-sales groups, depreciation, interest, taxes or other income. Preserve those distinctions when calculating metrics, and ask an accountant how unusual items should be classified. The results below illustrate a method; they are not universal targets for every industry, stage or business model.

1. Revenue trend: is demand actually moving?

Revenue trend measures how sales change across comparable periods. For a month-over-month view, use (current revenue - previous revenue) / previous revenue. In the example, revenue increased by $8,000, from $92,000 to $100,000. The growth rate is $8,000 / $92,000, or approximately 8.7%.

One comparison is not enough. Month-over-month movement catches recent changes, while the same month last year helps account for recurring seasonality. Budget comparison tests whether demand matched the plan. Where practical, separate price, volume, customer mix and one-time sales. Revenue can rise because the business served more customers, raised prices or landed a single unusual contract; those causes imply different next steps.

A warning pattern is falling revenue across several comparable periods, or revenue growth driven by discounting, a concentrated customer or low-quality work. The appropriate action is not automatically to spend more on marketing. First identify the source: fewer leads, lower conversion, churn, capacity constraints, delayed billing or a weaker sales mix. Assign an owner to the most plausible driver and choose one measurable response, such as contacting at-risk customers, revising an offer or removing a delivery bottleneck.

2. Gross margin: are sales becoming more valuable?

Gross profit is revenue minus cost of sales. Gross margin expresses that amount as a percentage: (Revenue - Cost of sales) / Revenue. For the sample month, gross profit is $100,000 - $40,000 = $60,000. Therefore, Gross margin = ($100,000 - $40,000) / $100,000 = 60%. In plain language, 60 cents of each revenue dollar remains after the direct costs of delivering the product or service, before operating overhead.

Last month, gross profit was $92,000 - $34,000 = $58,000, and gross margin was about 63.0%. Gross profit dollars rose by $2,000 this month, but gross margin declined by roughly three percentage points. Possible causes include supplier price increases, overtime, waste, discounting, an unfavorable product mix or direct costs coded inconsistently. The useful question is not merely whether 60% is good; it is why the business retained less of each sales dollar than last month.

Inspect the largest cost-of-sales changes and trace them to transactions or operational measures. A service business might compare contractor hours with billable work; a retailer might review purchasing, shrinkage and product mix; a restaurant might examine food and direct labor. Owners with multiple sites can see how this logic is applied in the multi-location restaurant P&L example. Respond with a targeted decision: renegotiate a supplier, change pricing, correct waste or improve scheduling, rather than an arbitrary company-wide cut.

3. Operating expense ratio: is overhead growing faster than the business?

Operating expenses are the costs of running the business that are not classified as direct cost of sales, such as administrative payroll, rent, software, marketing and professional fees. The operating expense ratio is Operating expenses / Revenue. In the example, Operating expense ratio = $48,000 / $100,000 = 48%.

Last month, the ratio was $44,000 / $92,000, or about 47.8%. It is nearly flat even though operating expenses increased by $4,000. That does not make every expense justified; it shows overhead broadly kept pace with revenue. Review both dollars and percentages because a ratio can hide a large cash commitment, while a dollar increase can be reasonable when it supports durable growth.

Group expenses into meaningful categories and compare each with budget. Separate fixed commitments, such as rent, from variable or discretionary spending, such as campaigns or temporary help. Investigate material variances rather than every small fluctuation. A warning pattern is an expense category rising faster than revenue for several periods without a clear return. The action may be to cancel unused software, reset hiring timing, change a campaign or renegotiate a contract. Do not cut capacity that generates profitable revenue simply to improve one month's percentage.

4. Profit margin: what remains after operating the business?

The SEC financial-statements guide describes operating profit as the amount remaining after operating expenses are deducted from gross profit and before interest and income tax expenses. Operating profit margin is Operating profit / Revenue. For the sample month, Operating profit margin = $12,000 / $100,000 = 12%. The P&L also reconciles directly: $100,000 revenue - $40,000 cost of sales - $48,000 operating expenses = $12,000 operating profit.

Last month's operating profit margin was $14,000 / $92,000, or about 15.2%. This month produced $8,000 more revenue but $2,000 less operating profit because combined costs increased by $10,000. The margin declined by about 3.2 percentage points. That is a stronger decision signal than revenue growth on its own and directs attention back to the gross-margin decline and the $4,000 increase in operating expenses.

Use this margin to judge the combined effect of pricing, delivery economics and overhead. Review recurring operations separately from genuinely unusual events, but do not relabel ordinary overspending as one-time every month. The next action should address the driver already identified: protect price, fix direct-cost leakage, slow an overhead commitment or build a realistic sales plan. The sample's 12% is illustrative, not a universal healthy target. Capital intensity, owner compensation, business maturity and industry economics all affect an appropriate margin.

5. Cash coverage: can the business fund the next few months?

Cash coverage is not a line on the P&L. It is a companion metric sourced from available cash and upcoming cash obligations. The P&L reports economic performance over a period; cash coverage asks how long the business can pay bills as they fall due. A profitable month can coexist with weak liquidity, so the monthly routine is incomplete until profit is connected to cash.

A simple runway calculation is available unrestricted cash / average monthly net cash outflow. If a business has $90,000 available and is consuming $30,000 per month, it has about three months of runway. If cash flow is positive, a runway ratio based on burn is less useful; instead, compare cash plus expected collections with payroll, taxes, debt service and supplier payments by date. Use current bank balances and a forward schedule, not the $12,000 operating profit from this example as a substitute for cash.

For a more actionable view, build a rolling 13-week cash-flow forecast. It reveals timing risks that a monthly P&L cannot: a large receivable arriving after payroll, inventory purchased before a seasonal peak, quarterly taxes, loan principal or an owner distribution. Decide whether to accelerate collections, defer optional spending, arrange financing or preserve a minimum cash buffer before the shortfall becomes urgent.

What should you review daily, weekly and monthly?

The monthly P&L belongs inside a layered routine. Frequent checks should be narrow and operational; monthly checks should be complete enough to support structural decisions.

CadenceReviewDecision
DailyCash and urgent obligationsProtect liquidity
WeeklySales, collections and major variable costsCorrect operating drift
MonthlyFull P&L, budget and prior-year comparisonChange pricing, cost or hiring plans

Avoid turning daily monitoring into daily strategy changes. A temporary sales dip or a vendor payment can create noise. Use weekly indicators to catch drift and monthly closed figures to evaluate trends, margins and larger commitments. Document which report, accounting basis and data owner support each cadence so the team compares the same definitions every time.

Common P&L reading mistakes

  • Treating revenue as cash. Credit sales can appear before collection, while deposits, loan proceeds and owner contributions can add cash without being revenue.
  • Reading one month in isolation. A single period cannot show whether a variance is a trend, timing issue or normal fluctuation.
  • Ignoring seasonality. A holiday business or contractor may look unusually strong or weak compared only with the previous month. Add the same period last year.
  • Confusing owner draws with operating expense. For a sole proprietor, IRS Publication 334 says personal withdrawals are not deductible business expenses and the owner is not an employee. Treatment differs by entity and arrangement, so confirm it with an accountant.
  • Using a generic benchmark without industry context. Cost structures vary. Compare first with the business's history, budget and operating model, then use relevant peer data as context rather than a verdict.

A ten-minute monthly P&L review

  1. Confirm the period and accounting method. Check that the month is closed enough to review, the cash or accrual basis is intentional and major entries are present.
  2. Compare revenue and margins. Read current month, prior month, prior year and budget. Calculate revenue trend, gross margin, operating expense ratio and operating profit margin.
  3. Inspect material variances. Focus on the few dollar or percentage changes large enough to alter a decision, and drill into their accounts or transactions.
  4. Connect profit to cash. Reconcile major timing differences and inspect the 13-week forecast, collections, payroll, taxes and debt obligations.
  5. Record one decision and one follow-up. Name the action, owner, due date and metric that will show whether it worked at the next review.

Ten minutes is realistic when classifications are consistent and reports refresh automatically. If most of the meeting is spent exporting, mapping accounts or debating which number is current, improve the reporting process before adding more metrics.

FAQ

How often should a small business review its P&L?

Review a complete P&L every month after the books are reasonably closed. Monitor a smaller set of operating indicators weekly and cash obligations daily. Highly seasonal or fast-moving businesses may produce a weekly flash P&L, but it should complement, not replace, the consistent monthly statement used for budget and prior-year comparisons.

What is the most important number on a P&L?

No single number works without context. Revenue shows demand but not the value of sales; gross margin shows unit economics but not overhead; operating profit shows the combined result but can hide cash timing. Read the measures as a chain. In the example, rising revenue looks positive until lower gross margin and higher costs explain why operating profit fell.

Why can a profitable business run out of cash?

Profit and cash can move at different times. Under accrual accounting, the IRS explains that income is generally reported when earned regardless of when payment arrives, while expenses are generally reported when incurred regardless of when they are paid. That timing alone means reported profit need not match the movement in the bank balance. Pair the P&L with receivables, payables, debt schedules and a cash forecast; the SBA financial-management guide specifically recommends preparing a cash-flow projection.

Should I compare against last month or last year?

Use both, plus budget. Last month highlights recent momentum, while the same month last year helps account for seasonality. Budget shows whether results matched the operating plan. If the comparisons disagree, investigate the reason. For example, revenue may rise month over month while remaining below the seasonal plan.

Turn the review into a repeatable report

A good monthly review ends with fewer surprises and one accountable decision, not a larger spreadsheet. Keep definitions consistent, automate comparisons, retain drill-down to source transactions and place P&L ratios beside cash and budget context. FinBoard's financial analytics brings trends, budget variances and operating detail into a repeatable reporting view, so owners can spend the meeting deciding what to change rather than rebuilding the numbers.
